root_cause: EffectComposer inconsistently stores _width/_height as logical pixels when no renderTarget is provided, but as physical pixels when a custom renderTarget is provided. This causes subsequent setSize() calls to double-multiply by pixelRatio, resulting in incorrect render target and pass dimensions.

RenderPass does not update the camera's aspect ratio and projection matrix when EffectComposer.setSize() is called, causing the scene to render with incorrect aspect ratio into the resized render target.

When EffectComposer resizes its internal render targets via setSize() or reset(), external shader uniforms that reference those render targets are not automatically updated. The blend shader's tDiffuse2 sampler uniform continues pointing to the old render target texture, causing the effects content

EffectComposer constructor assigns physical pixel dimensions to internal _width/_height fields that are treated as logical dimensions throughout the codebase, causing size calculation errors when custom renderTargets are provided.

The fragment shader uniform `transformation_matrix` is declared without initialization, causing GLSL to default it to a zero matrix. Multiplying any UV coordinate by a zero matrix yields (0, 0), so every fragment samples the top-left pixel of the source texture, producing a solid fill of that pixel'

The transformation_matrix uniform is declared in the fragment shader but never initialized by CPU code via glUniformMatrix2fv, causing it to default to the zero matrix per GLSL spec, which collapses all UV coordinates to (0,0).

The transformation_matrix uniform in the fragment shader is declared without a default value and never set by the CPU code path, causing GLSL to initialize it to a zero matrix. This transforms all texture UVs to (0,0), sampling only the top-left pixel.
The transformation_matrix uniform is declared in the fragment shader but never set by the CPU, defaulting to a zero matrix per GLSL spec, which collapses all UV coordinates to (0,0).

The separable blur shader in UnrealBloomPass only samples RGB channels and hardcodes the output alpha to 1.0, destroying transparency information during the bloom blur process. This causes the final composited result to be opaque instead of preserving the original alpha channel.

The separable blur shader in _getSeparableBlurMaterial hardcodes the alpha channel to 1.0 in its fragment shader output, destroying any transparency information from the input texture during the Gaussian blur passes.

The _getSeparableBlurMaterial fragment shader (line 441 of UnrealBloomPass.js) hardcodes the output alpha channel to 1.0 instead of sampling and preserving alpha from the input texture. This destroys transparency during the Gaussian blur passes, causing fully opaque bloom results that completely mas
The separable blur shader in UnrealBloomPass hardcodes the output alpha channel to 1.0, converting transparent background pixels to opaque black during blur passes.

FullScreenQuad's mesh uses default frustumCulled=true, causing the renderer to cull the triangle geometry whose vertices extend to (3,3) which lies outside the OrthographicCamera's [-1,1] frustum bounds.

FullScreenQuad uses module-level shared _camera and _geometry objects from Pass.js's THREE instance, which the renderer from 3d-force-graph's bundled THREE instance cannot properly process due to cross-instance module state mismatch.

LuminosityHighPassShader uses the luminance() function without including the <common> shader chunk that defines it, causing silent shader compilation failure. Three.js doesn't throw on shader compile errors - it logs warnings and renders nothing.

LuminosityHighPassShader uses undefined GLSL function luminance() that requires runtime injection by WebGLProgram, which fails when ShaderMaterial and WebGLRenderer are from different Three.js module instances, causing silent shader compilation failure.

The MeshDepthMaterial's depth visualization (gl_FragColor) is computed from raw projected depth (gl_Position.zw) without applying logarithmic depth correction. While the shader includes logdepthbuf support that corrects gl_FragDepth, it doesn't affect the color output used for visualization, causing
MeshDepthMaterial uses non-linear perspective depth buffer values directly from fragCoordZ, causing extreme compression with high near/far ratios. Linear depth formula exists but isn't applied.

The perspective projection matrix coefficients become large when near/far planes are close together, causing floating-point precision loss in the computation of gl_Position.z through catastrophic cancellation of nearly equal values during subtraction (c*viewZ + d where c=-12, d=-7150).
MeshDepthMaterial outputs perspective-correct depth (1/z distribution) instead of linear depth, causing non-uniform depth value distribution that the user explicitly wants to avoid.

The transparent point sprites issue is caused by PointsMaterial not automatically setting depthWrite=false for transparent materials, causing the depth buffer to be written and preventing back-to-front blending. The horizontal lines issue is caused by WebGL point sprite rasterization artifacts when
PointsMaterial defaults to depthWrite=true even when transparent=true, causing transparent sprites to incorrectly occlude each other instead of blending properly

offending_symbol: Material.depthWrite default value in constructor, and WebGLState.setDepthMask() applying it without transparency check
root_cause: PointsMaterial with transparent=true still has depthWrite=true (Material class default). Three.js doesn't automatically disable depth writing for transparent materials, causing transparent fragments to block underlying sprites while still being alpha-blended.

Three.js PointsMaterial does not automatically disable depth writing when transparent=true is set, causing overlapping transparent sprites to fail depth testing and show background instead of blending with underlying sprites.

MaskFilter.apply() does not properly synchronize the texture matrix when a pooled texture is reused with different dimensions. The mapCoord and uClampFrame are calculated before the sprite's texture state is fully committed, causing texture coordinate transformations to be calculated with stale matr
TextureMatrix setter uses object identity check that fails to detect when a pooled Texture object is reused with a different frame size, causing stale matrix calculations for subsequent masks

When multiple MaskFilters are applied with pooled textures via renderMaskToTexture, the _textureMatrix is not guaranteed to be updated before its mapCoord property is used in calculateSpriteMatrix().prepend(). The WebGL renderer does not automatically disable depthWrite for transparent materials with NormalBlending and DoubleSide. Front faces write to the depth buffer, causing back faces to fail the depth test and not render properly, which prevents objects inside the transparent object from showing throu

Transparent DoubleSide materials render front and back faces in two consecutive passes at the same sort depth, with depthWrite enabled, causing incorrect occlusion of objects that should appear between the faces.

Three.js does not automatically disable depth writes for transparent materials with blending enabled. When rendering both sides of transparent geometry (with DoubleSide), both faces write to the depth buffer, causing conflicting depth values that produce visual artifacts through failed depth tests.

Three.js materials default to depthWrite=true even for transparent materials; when rendering double-sided transparent geometry, both front and back faces write to the depth buffer in rasterization order, causing incorrect alpha blending artifacts instead of proper back-to-front transparency.

TexturePool.createTexture() did not explicitly set autoGenerateMipmaps, causing filter textures to inherit the global default and allocate unpopulated mipmap levels when users enabled mipmaps for text rendering.

GlTextureSystem calls generateMipmap() on empty render textures immediately after allocation, before any rendering occurs. These mipmaps are never regenerated after content is rendered to level 0, leaving them with corrupt data that filters sample when using scaled UV coordinates.

TexturePool-allocated render textures inherit autoGenerateMipmaps from global defaults, allocating full mipmap chains, but FilterSystem never generates mipmap data after rendering, leaving uninitialized levels that corrupt scaled UV sampling.

The backbuffer texture is created with a different resolution (backgroundResolution) than the input texture (filterData.resolution), causing texture coordinate mismatch. offending_symbol: Shader constructor group-99 fallback (Shader.ts:336-354, specifically line 348)
root_cause: GL-only filters assign uBackTexture to group 99 via Shader constructor fallback, but FilterSystem binds texture at group 0 index 3, causing uniform to point to wrong texture unit (0 instead of 3).

offending_symbol: FilterSystem._applyFiltersToTexture/setResource at line 877; Filter.__constructor resource registration at lines 217-221
root_cause: FilterSystem unconditionally binds to slot 3 for uBackTexture without validating that the filter shader actually registered that resource. When a custom filter shader uses uBackTexture but omits blendRequired:true, the resource layout mismatch causes shader binding failures or samplers pointing to w
FilterSystem binds backTexture source but not its sampler/style, causing uBackTexture to sample incorrectly

offending_symbol: meshnormal.glsl.js fragment shader lines 79-83 and WebGLPrograms.js line 252 opaque parameter logic
root_cause: MeshNormalMaterial's shader forces alpha to 1.0 when material.transparent is false (default), overriding the opacity property. Additionally, non-transparent materials don't use proper depth sorting for overlapping geometries, causing visual disappearance during rotation.

The blue cylinder material has opacity < 1 but lacks transparent: true, causing it to be classified as opaque and write to the depth buffer, which blocks rendering of the inner yellow cylinders at certain rotation angles.

offending_symbol: Material.js constructor (transparent default = false) and WebGLState.js setBlending logic
root_cause: Material.transparent must be explicitly set to true to enable alpha blending. The opacity property alone does not trigger transparency; without the transparent flag, blending is disabled and opacity only affects color, causing depth buffer artifacts.

User set opacity: 0.5 but forgot transparent: true. Three.js requires BOTH properties for transparent rendering; without transparent: true, materials are treated as opaque regardless of opacity value.

In prior versions, the _colorMatrix() method incorrectly divided offset values by 255 only when multiply=true, creating asymmetry between multiply modes. This caused offsets to become nearly zero (e.g., -0.5 → -0.002), eliminating the contrast adjustment while preserving the scale factor, resulting
The _colorMatrix method incorrectly divided offset values by 255 only when multiply=true, breaking contrast and other filters that used normalized offset values in the 0-1 range.

The _colorMatrix method was only called when multiply=true, dividing offset values by 255. This caused matrix offsets to be inconsistently handled: multiply=false uses raw offsets (e.g., -0.5), while multiply=true incorrectly normalized them (e.g., -0.5/255≈-0.002), making color adjustments like con

The _colorMatrix method divided color matrix offsets by 255 only when multiply=true, but contrast() already provided offsets in 0-1 range (-0.5), causing the offset to become ~255x smaller (-0.00196) and making contrast mostly brighten colors instead of adjusting contrast.

The getTangentFrame function in normalmap_pars_fragment.glsl.js incorrectly normalizes computed tangent and bitangent vectors using max(|T|², |B|²) instead of the true 2D determinant. When shadow-casting lights create boundaries aligned with 2x2 pixel quad discontinuities, T and B magnitudes differ

The anisotropic BRDF functions V_GGX_SmithCorrelated_Anisotropic and D_GGX_Anisotropic lack epsilon-based division-by-zero protection that exists in their non-anisotropic counterparts, causing NaN values when tangent frame calculations produce edge cases with problematic UV derivatives.

In D_GGX_Anisotropic function, alphaT and alphaB are swapped when computing the tangent/bitangent distribution, causing incorrect specular calculation: 'alphaB * dotTH, alphaT * dotBH' should be 'alphaT * dotTH, alphaB * dotBH'.
Anisotropic BRDF functions receive isotropic roughness (alpha) instead of missing alphaB parameter, causing incorrect lighting calculations that produce black artifacts.

root_cause: The Texture constructor's update() method crashes with 'Cannot read property width of undefined' when texture.image is undefined. Node transformation matrices from GLTF are not converted from right-handed (+Y up) to left-handed (+Z up) coordinate system, causing incorrect rendering for models with node hierarchies

If a previous draw call set depthMask=true, it persists, leaving the GPU in a state with depth testing disabled but depth writing enabled, causing GLB models to render

The setDepthMode function in context.ts disables depth testing but fails to update depth mask state, leaving depth writes enabled while depth testing is disabled, causing incorrect 3D model rendering without proper depth sorting.

The patchUpdatedImages method destructively filters haveRenderCallbacks array based on scope, permanently removing animated images when called with a scope that doesn't contain those images, even though the images were successfully included in the ImageAtlas during construction.

Context.setDepthMode() disables depth testing when mode is 'disabled' but fails to update the depth mask, leaving it at the previous render pass's value. Symbols incorrectly write depth with testing disabled, causing mutual occlusion.

Downstream effects read ga

OverrideMaterialManager only applies override materials to Mesh objects via scene.overrideMaterial or explicit isMesh checks, leaving Sprites/Points with incompatible single-output materials when MRT gbuffer rendering is active.

offending_symbol: OverrideMaterialManager.replaceMaterial and material shader compilation when MRT is active
root_cause: Fragment shaders for non-PBR materials (PointsMaterial, SpriteMaterial) omit layout(location=1) output declarations when rendering to an MRT-enabled framebuffer with both color and normal attachments active, leaving the normal buffer undefined at those pixels.

OverrideMaterialManager only processes node.isMesh objects, excluding Sprites and Points. When scene.overrideMaterial is set to MeshNormalMaterial, THREE.js skips rendering these incompatible object types.

In v6.38.1, EffectMaterial blending was changed from NoBlending to NormalBlending, and material.transparent was dynamically set based on canvas alpha. This combination causes improper alpha blending during opacity animations on alpha canvases, producing black glitches. The fix reverted to NoBlending

Version 6.38.2 removed alpha-aware transparency handling when rendering to screen, causing premultiplied alpha output with NoBlending to create black artifacts during opacity animation on alpha-enabled canvases.

When rendering alpha canvas to screen, the code sets material.transparent=true while blending is NoBlending, causing THREE.js to set both WebGL blend factors to GL_ZERO, resulting in final_color = src*0 + dst*0 = black artifacts during opacity animation.
EffectMaterial switched from NoBlending to NormalBlending, causing OpenGL alpha blending to interfere with shader-based opacity blending during fade animations on alpha canvas.

The CircleOfConfusionMaterial shader incorrectly assumes BasicDepthPacking stores NDC depth coordinates, when it actually stores linearized distance values in [0,1]. The shader applies a linear conversion (depth * 2.0 - 1.0) instead of the nonlinear perspective projection formula, causing incorrect

The shader's perspectiveDepthToViewZ uses camera near/far parameters that don't match the near/far values active when the depth buffer was rendered, causing incorrect depth-to-viewZ conversion and view position reconstruction.

The CircleOfConfusionMaterial uses Euclidean distance (length of 3D view position) instead of Z-distance for depth of field calculations, causing off-axis pixels to blur more than expected and making the focus plane appear shallower.

The default PerspectiveCamera near=0.1 creates excessive near/far ratios that collapse depth buffer precision. The non-linear perspective depth mapping allocates virtually all 24-bit precision to the near frustum, making distant non-coplanar geometry indistinguishable.

Depth buffer precision is insufficient due to the default PerspectiveCamera near/far ratio (0.1/2000 = 1:20000). When zooming out to view large geometry, objects separated by small distances cannot be accurately depth-sorted, causing z-fighting artifacts.

Z-fighting caused by insufficient depth precision in standard 24-bit linear depth buffer with large camera frustum ratio (far/near ≈ 300,000:1). The perspective projection matrix concentrates precision near the near plane, making geometrically-close objects at distance indistinguishable.

Insufficient depth buffer precision due to large far/near plane ratio (~2000:1) in perspective projection, causing depth quantization that cannot distinguish objects separated by 1 unit at similar depths
